sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP, UP, UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 10, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Blocks moved UP to rows 35-39, cols 34-38. The gray corridor continues upward. I can keep moving UP toward the target.
- Recent findings: After RIGHT then UP, blocks are at rows 40-44, cols 39-43. Need to find the path to the target at the top.
- Plan: Batch multiple UP moves to reach the target area at rows 9-15.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 9 of about 22 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The blocks moved up 5 times. Now I can see the orange block is right below the target area (the black box with the blue shape). The blocks seem to be at rows 10-14, cols 34-38. The target is at rows 9-15. Let me check the exact position and see if I need to move UP one more time or if the blocks are already at the target.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current block position
after = current_frame

print("=== Orange block ===")
for n in after.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        print(f"  boundary={n['boundary']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

print("\n=== Blue block ===")
for n in after.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 15:
        print(f"  boundary={n['boundary']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check the target area
print("\n=== Target area (rows 8-16, cols 28-44) ===")
ascii_lines = after.ascii.split('\n')
for r in range(8, 17):
    row_str = ""
    for c in range(28, 45):
        ch = ascii_lines[r][c]
        if ch == 'c': row_str += '.'
        elif ch == 'G': row_str += '#'
        elif ch == 'O': row_str += 'o'
        elif ch == 'b': row_str += 'b'
        elif ch == 'B': row_str += 'B'
        else: row_str += ch
    print(f"{r:2d}: {row_str}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The orange block is now at rows 15-16, cols 34-38, and the blue block is at rows 17-19, cols 34-38. Looking at the target area:
- Row 15: `#BoooooB#` - The orange block is inside the black box!
- Row 16: `##ooooo##` - The orange block extends below the box
- The blue block is at rows 17-19, below the box
The target blue shape is at rows 11-13 inside the black box. The orange block is at rows 15-16, partially inside the box.
It seems like the blocks need to be positioned so they match the target shape. The target is a blue L-shape at rows 11-13. The current blocks are orange (2 rows) + blue (3 rows) = 5 rows total.
Wait, looking at the target more carefully:
- Row 11: `BBbbbBB` - blue shape at cols 32-36
- Row 12: `BBBBbBB` - blue shape at cols 34-34 (just 1 cell)
- Row 13: `BBbBbBB` - blue shape at cols 32-32 and 34-34
So the target is a pattern of blue cells inside the black box. The blocks need to match this pattern.
But the blocks are currently orange and blue, not matching the target pattern. Maybe the goal is to move the blocks into the target area and they'll change color or match the pattern?
Actually, looking at the image, the orange block is visible inside the black box at row 15. The blue block is below. The target blue shape is at rows 11-13.
I think I need to move the blocks UP further so they're fully inside the target box. Let me try moving UP one more time.
Wait, but the orange block is already at rows 15-16. Moving UP would put it at rows 10-11, which is inside the target box. The blue block is at rows 17-19, moving UP would put it at rows 12-14, also inside the box.
Let me try UP.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: The orange block is at rows 15-16 and the blue block at rows 17-19, partially inside the target box (rows 9-15). The target blue shape is at rows 11-13. I need to move the blocks UP so they're fully inside the target box.
Plan: Move UP one more time to position the blocks inside the target area.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move UP one more time to get blocks fully into the target box
action(['UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
